model.description: MiMo • The flagship foundation for the agentic era, built for complex, long-horizon, multi-step agentic tasks.

pick.rationale: UConn holds a slight edge as the higher seed (2 vs 3) with a 29-5 record and elite defensive efficiency under Dan Hurley, while Michigan State's tournament experience under Tom Izzo is a counterweight. The Huskies' pre-season scrimmage victory over the Spartans and their Big East championship pedigree provide a psychological advantage, though Michigan State's balanced attack and defensive prowess make this a tightly contested matchup. UConn's overall talent depth and ability to control tempo should prove decisive in a competitive Sweet 16 game.

reasoningStep.title: Michigan State vs UConn Sweet 16 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Evaluated both teams' tournament paths, injury status, and matchup dynamics to determine UConn as a slight favorite.
reasoningStep.evidence: UConn is the 2-seed with a 29-5 record and Big East championship, while Michigan State is the 3-seed with a 25-7 record. UConn's pre-season scrimmage win over Michigan State and their elite defensive efficiency under Dan Hurley provide an edge. Michigan State's tournament experience under Tom Izzo and balanced attack keep the matchup close, but UConn's overall talent depth and ability to control tempo give them a narrow advantage. Injury updates show both teams relatively healthy, with UConn's Silas Demary Jr. in good shape and Michigan State's key players available.

result.text: Michigan State’s Sweet 16 chances get a boost after Louisville injury update - mlive.com Skip to Article Set weather Back To Main Menu Close Quick Look - Change your current location » Current - Mostly Sunny Tonight Low - Mostly Sunny Tomorrow High - Mostly Sunny View full weather report Ann Arbor Flint Grand Rapids/Muskegon Jackson Kalamazoo Saginaw/Bay City All Michigan One subscription. Five Users. Start Premium Access for $1 Basketball Michigan State’s Sweet 16 chances get a boost after Louisville injury update Updated: Mar. 18, 2026, 12:47 p.m. | Published: Mar. 18, 2026, 12:42 p.m. Louisville guard Mikel Brown Jr. walks off the court after his team lost to North Carolina in an NCAA college basketball game Monday, Feb. 23, 2026, in Chapel Hill, N.C. (AP Photo/Chris Seward) AP By Jacob Richman | jrichman@mlive.com Michigan State’s path to a deep run in the NCAA Tournament is looking a bit clearer as their potential second-round opponent is going to be without a key player for opening weekend. While the Spartans need to get past 14 seed North Dakota State (27-7) first , a meeting with 6 seed Louisville is the expected matchup in the Round of 32. But, the school announced Wednesday that star freshman Mikel Brown Jr. won’t be available due to a back injury. The former McDonald’s All-American and third-team All-ACC selection missed the final two games of the regular season and Louisville’s conference tournament run, where it was eliminated by Miami in the quarterfinals. An expected lottery pick in the 2026 NBA Draft, Brown has struggled with back issues for much of the season, but still managed to have a huge year, averaging 18.2 points, 4.7 assists, 3.3 blocks and 1.2 steals per game, while shooting 41% from the field and 34.4% from distance. In total, Brown has missed 12 games and the Cardinals are 7-5 without him. The Spartans could keep Brown from getting to play another college game if they reach the second round and knock out the Cardinals, who are 23-10 this season. It’s expected Brown enters the NBA as a one-and-done, already looking like a pro-level backcourt playmaker. MSU last met Louisville in the NCAA Tournament in 2015 in the Elite Eight. The Spartans won that matchup in overtime before losing to Duke in the Final Four as a 7-seed. Their last meeting came in 2021 for a nonconference matchup in East Lansing, which Michigan State also won. In order to set up that meeting, the Spartans need to take care of business on Thursday in Buffalo, New York against the Summit League champions and Louisville will have to best 11-seed South Florida (25-8). The champions of the American, the Bulls made a wild turnaround in Year 1 under coach Bryan Hodgson going from 13-19 to the program’s first March Madness bid since 2012. USF enters the tournament on an 11-game win streak. While the Spartans were bounced from the Big Ten Tournament early, they’re still considered a threat to make a deep run in the Big Dance from the East Region. LATEST MSU BASKET

Joe Buglewicz/Getty Images The UConn men's basketball team prepares for its upcoming NCAA Tournament run potentially low on manpower with the injury statuses of two key contributors still in question ahead of Friday's opener against Furman . Jaylin Stewart has not played since Feb. 21, as the junior wing continues to recover from an unspecified knee injury, while Silas Demary Jr. was forced to exit Saturday's Big East Tournament final with a left ankle sprain . Dan Hurley provided updates on both players while speaking with the media Sunday evening. Advertisement Article continues below this ad "Health-wise, it will be close with Jaylin," Hurley said. The UConn coach provided a more promising update on his star point guard: "I think Silas is in pretty good shape." Jaylin Stewart has faced a mercurial junior season, starting UConn's first 10 games this season as freshman wing Braylon Mullins battled a pre-season injury — the Seattle native delivered double-digit scoring performances against Arizona and Texas. Stewart returned to an off-the-bench role once Mullins hit his stride around the New Year, but he stepped up with a crucial pair of 3-pointers against Villanova in his final game before the ongoing injury saga. More For You Kayleigh Heckel lost to UConn in March Madness last year. Now she's a Husky The Huskies still tease Heckel about fouling Paige Bueckers in last year's Elite Eight against USC. Michael Hickey / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated March 18, 2026 6:32 pm EDT Share full article 3 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Mikel Brown Jr., Louisville Brown will miss the first weekend of the NCAA Tournament — including Thursday’s first-round game against No. 11 South Florida — because of a lingering back injury, the school announced. The highly talented Brown missed the Cardinals’ final four games with the injury, which also kept him out of eight contests in December and January. “I’ve been working out, been doing a lot of physical stuff, you know, bumping, guarding, sprints up and down the floor. result.text: UConn's Dan Hurley gives injury updates before NCAA Tournament Ct Insider Logo Hearst Newspapers Logo Skip to main content Subscribe Home Contact Us Advertise With Us Newsletters FAQ Privacy Notice Terms of Use E-Edition Our Brands Cheshire Herald Connecticut Magazine County Times Fairfield Citizen Journal Inquirer New Milford Spectrum Record-Journal Shoreline Times West Hartford News Westport News Waterbury Republican-American News Education Politics Election Health Latino News Sports GametimeCT UConn UConn Women's Basketball UConn Men's Basketball College Connecticut Sun WNBA NFL NBA MLB Hockey Business Entertainment Horoscopes Advice Puzzles Comics Real Estate Living Food Weather Opinion We Asked, You Answered Obits Best Of Education Home Services Legal Services Senior Living Entertainment Food & Drink Health Advertising Features Events Jobs Classifieds Ct Insider Logo Subscribe Trending: Chilly Wednesday | Off-duty police in schools? | UConn's Serah Williams | Leaf blower debate | Horoscopes Sports // UConn Men's Basketball Dan Hurley gives injury updates on UConn's Jaylin Stewart, Silas Demary Jr. ahead of NCAA Tournament By Declan Walsh , Staff Writer March 16, 2026 STORRS, CONNECTICUT - NOVEMBER 19: Silas Demary Jr. #2 and Jaylin Stewart #3 of the Connecticut Huskies dive for the loose ball against Brayden Burries #5 of the Arizona Wildcats during the first half of an NCAA men's basketball game. Joe Buglewicz/Getty Images The UConn men's basketball team prepares for its upcoming NCAA Tournament run potentially low on manpower with the injury statuses of two key contributors still in question ahead of Friday's opener against Furman . Jaylin Stewart has not played since Feb. 21, as the junior wing continues to recover from an unspecified knee injury, while Silas Demary Jr. was forced to exit Saturday's Big East Tournament final with a left ankle sprain . Dan Hurley provided updates on both players while speaking with the media Sunday evening. Advertisement Article continues below this ad "Health-wise, it will be close with Jaylin," Hurley said. The UConn coach provided a more promising update on his star point guard: "I think Silas is in pretty good shape." Jaylin Stewart has faced a mercurial junior season, starting UConn's first 10 games this season as freshman wing Braylon Mullins battled a pre-season injury — the Seattle native delivered double-digit scoring performances against Arizona and Texas. Stewart returned to an off-the-bench role once Mullins hit his stride around the New Year, but he stepped up with a crucial pair of 3-pointers against Villanova in his final game before the ongoing injury saga. More For You Kayleigh Heckel lost to UConn in March Madness last year. Now she's a Husky The Huskies still tease Heckel about fouling Paige Bueckers in last year's Elite Eight against USC. Michael Hickey / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated March 18, 2026 6:32 pm EDT Share full article 3 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Mikel Brown Jr., Louisville Brown will miss the first weekend of the NCAA Tournament — including Thursday’s first-round game against No. 11 South Florida — because of a lingering back injury, the school announced. The highly talented Brown missed the Cardinals’ final four games with the injury, which also kept him out of eight contests in December and January. “I’ve been working out, been doing a lot of physical stuff, you know, bumping, guarding, sprints up and down the floor.
